cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Use willSet and didSet to print `Will set to <newValue>` before a change and `Did set from <oldValue>` after a change.
expectedOutput:
Will set to 2
Did set from 1
hints: Observers can print before and after changes.
Use 'newValue' inside 'willSet' to see the value that is about to be set.
Use 'oldValue' inside 'didSet' to see the value that was replaced.
title: Property Observers

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
hints: Lazy properties are created the first time they are accessed.
A lazy property must always be declared as a variable (using 'var'), because its initial value might not be retrieved until after instance initialization completes.
The value of a lazy property is only calculated once, the first time it is used.
lesson:
Lesson: Lazy Properties
A lazy stored property is a property whose initial value is not calculated until the first time it is used. This is useful for properties whose initial values are expensive to compute or depend on external factors.

Key rules:
1. You must always declare a lazy property as a variable (using the 'var' keyword).
2. Lazy properties are useful for deferring work until it's actually needed.

Example:
    struct DataManager {
        lazy var importer = DataImporter() // Only created when accessed
        var data: [String] = []
    }

Note: If a property marked with 'lazy' is accessed by multiple threads simultaneously and the property has not yet been initialized, there is no guarantee that the property will be initialized only once.
title: Lazy Property

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Use static properties to share values across instances
hints: Static properties live on the type itself, not on any one instance.
Access a static property by prefixing it with the type name: 'Shift.maxHours'.
Instance properties are accessed via an instance of the type: 'shift.hours'.
lesson:
Lesson: Type Properties (static)
Properties usually belong to instances of a type. However, you can also define 'type properties' that belong to the type itself.

Core Concepts:
1. static keyword: Marks a property as belonging to the type. There is only ever one copy, regardless of how many instances you create.
2. Access: Use the type name (e.g., 'Shift.maxHours') to read or write type properties.
3. Universal Values: Use static properties for constants or variables that should be shared by every instance (like a max limit, a shared cache, or a default configuration).

Example:
    struct Settings {
        static let apiVersion = "1.0"
        var username: String
    }
    print(Settings.apiVersion) // Access via Type
title: Static vs Instance

hints: Protocols define required properties or methods.
A read-only property in a protocol is defined as 'var status: String { get }'.
Protocols do not have curly braces for implementations, just the signature followed by a semicolon or line break (for methods) or the { get } / { get set } requirements.
title: Protocol Definition

description: Adopt a protocol in a struct
hints: Provide the required property to conform.
The struct 'Furnace' should be declared as 'struct Furnace: Inspectable'.
Since the protocol requires 'status', your struct must have a property or computed property named 'status'.
title: Conformance

description: Use a protocol as a parameter type
hints: Protocol types let you accept a conforming type.
The parameter 'source' in your function should be of type 'HeatSource'.
Because the function accepts the protocol, it can access any properties defined in that protocol, such as 'heat'.
title: Protocol as Type

description: Use A & B in a parameter
hints: Use protocol composition to require both capabilities.
The type of the parameter should be 'Fueling & Venting'.
Inside the function, you can access properties from both protocols on the single parameter.
title: Protocol Composition

hints: Protocol inheritance adds requirements on top of another protocol.
Define 'InspectableComponent' like this: 'protocol InspectableComponent: Component { ... }'.
Types that conform to 'InspectableComponent' must implement requirements from both it AND the 'Component' protocol.
title: Protocol Inheritance

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
hints: Extensions can add computed properties and methods.
Use 'extension Ingot { ... }' to add your method.
The 'label()' method should return a string like "Ingot \(weight)".
lesson:
Lesson: Extensions
Extensions add new functionality to an existing type, even if you don't have access to the original source code.

What Extensions Can Do:
1. Add Methods: Group related utility functions outside the main type definition.
2. Computed Properties: Add derived state without adding stored data.
3. Protocol Conformance: Keep your code organized by putting protocol requirements in an extension.
4. Default Implementations: Use protocol extensions to provide 'free' functionality to any type that conforms.

Example:
    extension Int {
        var isEven: Bool { return self % 2 == 0 }
    }
    print(4.isEven) // true

Best Practices:
- Use extensions to break up large types into logical blocks.
- Don't use extensions to hide complexity; use them to organize it.
title: Extensions

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Add default behavior with a protocol extension
hints: Use a protocol extension to add a default method.
The extension should be defined as 'extension Reportable { ... }'.
Any type that conforms to 'Reportable' will now automatically have the 'printReport()' method.
